claimed_contribution: The paper develops an integrated statistical and optimization framework for Internet customization that personalizes both email content and layout at the individual level.
It introduces a Bayesian semiparametric MDP probit implementation for model-based collaborative filtering that jointly captures user, email, and link heterogeneity.
It provides an exact assignment-based optimization procedure that selects which links to include and where to place them in each customized email.
context: The application is permission-based outbound email marketing for a large, anonymous content Web site (described as one of the top-ten most trafficked sites). The firm sends editorial email links to registered users and aims to increase traffic back to the site through clicks.
design_and_data: The study uses a two-phase approach: first a random-utility click-through model is estimated, then predicted probabilities are used in combinatorial optimization of email design. Data combine email files and clickstream logs collected in 1999, with 1048 users available; model estimation uses a random sample of 100 users, with 11,436 calibration observations and two validation sets (Extant: 3,735; Novel: 3,900). The authors estimate and compare three models: no-heterogeneity probit, hierarchical normal random-effects probit, and a Dirichlet process (MDP) random-effects probit with user, email, and link effects. Optimization is solved exactly via linear assignment subproblems under objectives of maximizing expected clicks or probability of at least one click.
main_findings: Models that account for heterogeneity outperform the no-heterogeneity model, and the MDP model is favored over the normal random-effects model by pseudo-Bayes factors with slightly better ROC prediction.
Link position has a negative effect on click-through, the time-since-last-click variable is negative, and substantial unobserved heterogeneity exists across users and emails.

Customized targeting and design can substantially improve outcomes; in the reported extant validation setting, optimal customization can raise expected clicks by about 62 percent relative to the original configuration.
one_line_summary: Using clickstream and email data, the paper proposes an MDP-probit-plus-optimization system that customizes permission-based emails and shows large potential click-through gains.
relation_to_prior_work: extends one-to-one marketing and recommendation research by moving from recommending single items to jointly optimizing communication content and design, while embedding collaborative filtering in a Bayesian semiparametric heterogeneity model.
research_gap: The paper states that despite the Web's strong customization potential, few marketing models operationalize one-to-one Internet targeting at the individual level. It also argues that existing recommendation approaches focus on suggesting items and do not directly solve the joint content-selection and layout-design optimization problem for complex communications such as emails.
research_question: How can firms model individual click behavior on Internet communications and then optimally customize both the content and design of each permission-based email to increase site traffic? The paper also examines whether a semiparametric MDP representation of heterogeneity can improve prediction and support better customization than conventional normal random-effects models.
